title: W1 E Suggested Solutions
body:
Suggested Solutions for Week 1 Juan Carlos Carbajal

ECON 3202 — Term 1, 2026 UNSW Sydney

Problem 1. Let n ∈ N be an arbitrary odd number. Show that it can be written as the difference between the square of two positive integers. Solution Since n is odd, I can write it as n = 2k − 1, for some k = 1, 2, Now, adding and subtracting k2 obtains

n = 2k − 1 + k2 − k2 = k2 − (k2 − 2k + 1) = k 2 − ( k − 1)2 .

Letting m = k − 1 we see that n = k2 − m2 , as desired. △

Problem 2. Prove the following.

Proposition 1

For all n ∈ N, one has that n ( n + 1) 1 + 2 + ... + n = .

Solution Following the hint, assume first that n is an even number, which implies that n/2 is also a natural number. Now arrange the sum 1 + 2 + + n in two rows as follows:

n 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + 2 + n n + ( n − 1) + ( n − 2) + ... + ( 2 + 1)

You will realize that adding the first column gives n + 1. But so does adding the second column: (n − 1) + 2 = n + 1. In fact, adding each of the n/2 columns yields

the same result, namely n + 1. Thus, we have

n ( n + 1) 1 + 2 + + n = ( n + 1) + ( n + 1) + + ( n + 1) = . 󰁿 󰁾󰁽 󰂀 2 n/2 times

Assume now that n is an odd number. Then it follows that n − 1 is an even number, and thus

( n − 1) n n ( n + 1) 1 + 2 + + ( n − 1) + n = +n = . 2 2

Thus, for any n ∈ N, the statement of the proposition holds. △

Alternative Solution We use an induction argument. Let S(n) be the statement

n ( n + 1) 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = , for n ∈ N.

The induction argument consists of three steps. The first step in the induction argument shows that this statement is true for n = 1. The second step in the induction argument is to assume that the statement is true for some arbitrarily chosen k ∈ N, k ∕= 1. The final step in the induction argument is to show that, given that S(1) and S(k) are true, the statement S(k + 1) is true as well. Step 1. Note that on the left-hand side of S(1) we simply have 1. On the right-hand side of S(1) we have 1(1 + 1) 1×2 = = 1. 2 2 Hence S(1) is true. Note that it is also simple to verify that S(2) is true:

2×3 1+2 = = 3.

Step 2. By our inductive hypothesis, it is true that

k ( k + 1) 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + k = .

Step 3. We now evaluate the validity of S(k + 1). One has that

1 + 2 + 3 + + k + ( k + 1) = (1 + 2 + 3 + + k ) + ( k + 1) k ( k + 1) (k + 1)(k + 2) = + ( k + 1) = , 2 2

as required. Hence, by induction, S(n) is true for all n ∈ N. △

Problem 3. Prove the following lemma.

Lemma 1 Let S and T be any two sets.

(a) S = T if and only if S ⊆ T and T ⊆ S;

(b) If T is finite and S ⊆ T, then |S| ≤ | T |;

Note that the finiteness of T in part (b) is important. If T is infinite, it can contain a strict subset with the same number of elements. Can you think of an example?

Solution (a) "=⇒" Let x ∈ S. Then since S = T, x ∈ T. Therefore, S ⊆ T. Analogously, let y ∈ T. Since T = S, y ∈ S. Therefore T ⊆ S. "⇐=" Suppose that S ⊆ T and T ⊆ S, but to obtain a contradiction suppose S ∕= T. This gives us two possibilities. Either there is an element of S not in T, or there is an element in T not in S. (i) Suppose there is some y ∈ S, but y ∈ / T. Since S ⊆ T, y ∈ T, this is a contradiction. (ii) Suppose that there is some z ∈ T but z ∈ / S. Since T ⊆ S, therefore, z ∈ S. This is a contradiction.

(b) Let T = { x1 , x2 , · · · xm }. Therefore, | T | = m. There are two cases to analyze. Case 1: S = T. Then |S| = m Case 2: There is some x ∈ T \ S. Therefore, |S| ≤ m − 1 < m = | T |. In both cases, | S | ≤ | T |.

An example where a strict subset of a set has the same cardinality as the original set itself is the case of Q and N. The set of natural numbers N is a strict subset of the set of rational numbers Q, but both sets have the same cardinality: they are both countably infinite (sometimes mathematicians denote this by ℵ0 ). △

Problem 4. Prove that the distributive laws hold.

Lemma 2 Let X, Y, Z be arbitrary sets. Then:

(a) X ∪ (Y ∩ Z ) = ( X ∪ Y ) ∩ ( X ∪ Z );

(b) X ∩ (Y ∪ Z ) = ( X ∩ Y ) ∪ ( X ∩ Z ).

Solution (a) To obtain the first equality, I must show the following two implications:

X ∪ (Y ∩ Z ) ⊆ ( X ∪ Y ) ∩ ( X ∪ Z ) , (1) ( X ∪ Y ) ∩ ( X ∪ Z ) ⊆ X ∪ (Y ∩ Z ) . (2)

To show Equation 1, let s ∈ X ∪ (Y ∩ Z ). Then it must be that either s ∈ X or s ∈ Y ∩ Z. Note that one of these two possibilities must hold. • If s ∈ X we can conclude that s ∈ ( X ∪ Y ) and also s ∈ ( X ∪ Z ). Therefore s ∈ ( X ∪ Y ) ∩ ( X ∪ Z ), as desired. • If instead s ∈ Y ∩ Z, then it must be that s ∈ Y and s ∈ Z. Therefore, we conclude that s ∈ ( X ∪ Y ) and at the same time s ∈ ( X ∪ Z ), and hence s ∈ ( X ∪ Y ) ∩ ( X ∪ Z ). To show Equation 2, let s ∈ ( X ∪ Y ) ∩ ( X ∪ Z ). Then s belongs to ( X ∪ Y ) and at the same time s belongs to ( X ∪ Z ). Thus, one of the following cases must hold: • s ∈ X and s ∈ X, • s ∈ X and s ∈ Z, • s ∈ Y and s ∈ X, • s ∈ Y and s ∈ Z. From the first three, we can conclude that s ∈ X and thus s ∈ X ∪ (Y ∩ Z ). From the last one, we conclude that s ∈ (Y ∩ Z ) and thus s ∈ X ∪ (Y ∩ Z ), as desired.

(b) This can be showed in a manner similar to the one used to show part (a). △

Problem 5. This is an economy with n perfectly divisible goods. For any vector of prices p ≥ 0 and wealth level w ≥ 0, the consumer budget set is

B( p, w) = { x ∈ R n+ | p · x ≤ w}.

• At time t = 1, the consumer has wealth w1 > 0 and the price vector is p1 = ( p11 , , p1n ), where p1i > 0 for each good i = 1, , n.

• At time t = 2, the consumer wealth increases to w2 > w1 > 0, but the price vector is unchanged, so p2 = p1 ; i.e., p2i = p1i for each good i = 1, , n.

• At time t = 3, the consumer wealth remains the same (w3 = w2 ), and so does the price of every good but good 1; i.e., p3i = p2i > 0 for each i = 2, 3, , n. At this time, however, good 1 becomes more affordable: 0 < p31 < p21 .

• At time t = 4, wealth remains as in the previous period, and so does every price but the price of good 2. This good has become less affordable: 0 < p32 < p42 .

(a) Show that B( p1 , w1 ) ⊆ B( p2 , w2 ). In fact, show that the set inclusion is strict: there is at least one consumption bundle x in B( p2 , w2 ) that is not in B( p1 , w1 ). (b) Show that B( p2 , w2 ) ⊆ B( p3 , w3 ). In fact, show that the set inclusion is strict: there is at least one consumption bundle y in B( p3 , w3 ) that is not in B( p2 , w2 ). Note that, in this case, the bundle y cannot have zero consumption of good 1. (c) What can be say about B( p4 , w4 ) with respect to B( p3 , w3 )?

Solution (a) To show that B( p1 , w1 ) ⊆ B( p2 , w2 ), we must show that any element in the first set is also contained in the second one. Take any x ∈ B( p1 , w1 ). Then, by definition and using the fact that p1 = p2 and 0 < w1 < w2 , we can write n n p2 · x = ∑ p2i xi = ∑ p1i xi ≤ w1 < w2 . i =1 i =1

Thus, it follows that x ∈ B( p2 , w2 ), as desired. Note this set inclusion is strict. Any bundle of consumption goods x in the budget line for price vector p2 and wealth w2 is not affordable at the same prices (recall p1 = p2 ) and a strictly lower income (recall w1 < w2 ). (b) Similarly, we must show that any consumption bundle x in B( p2 , w2 ) must also be in B( p3 , w3 ). Remember w3 = w2 , p3i = p2i for all i = 2, , n and 0 < p31 < p21 . Thus, if x belongs to B( p2 , w2 ), we can write n p ·x = p31 x1 + ∑ p3i xi i =2 n ≤ p2i x1 + ∑ p2i xi ≤ w2 = w3 . i =2

It follows that x also belongs to B( p3 , w3 ). Notice that the first inequality in the above expression is weak, despite having p31 < p21 . This is because x1 could be zero. To show strict set inclusion, consider the consumption bundle y where the consumer spends all her income on good 1, and zero on all other goods: y = (y1 , 0, , 0). At prices and income combination ( p3 , w3 ), she can afford y1 = w3 /p31 . But this bundle is unaffordable at prices and income combination ( p2 , w2 ):

w3 p21 2 p2 · y = p21 y1 = p21 = w > w2 , p31 p31

where the last strict inequality follows from the fact that p21 > p31 > 0, and thus p21 /p31 > 1. (c) This is similar to part (b) and thus omitted. △

Problem 6. Consider two vectors x = ( x1 , , xn ) and y = (y1 , , yn ) in R n . Say that x is weakly greater than y, written x ≥ y, if and only if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, , n. Say that x is strictly greater than y, written x > y, if and only if xi > yi for all i = 1, , n.

(a) Show for any x, y and z in R n , x ≥ y and y ≥ z implies x ≥ z. In words, the weakly greater than relation is transitive. (b) Show for any x, y and z in R n , x > y and y > z implies x > z. (c) Is is true that if x ≥ y and y > z, then x > z?

Solution (a) Clearly, for each i = 1, , n we have xi ≥ yi and yi ≥ zi , which implies xi ≥ zi since these are all real numbers and the greater than relation in R is transitive. Thus, it follows that x ≥ z, as desired.

(b) The argument is the similar and thus omitted.

(c) Suppose x ≥ y and y > z. Then, for all i = 1, , n, we have xi ≥ yi and yi > zi . It follows xi > zi , for all i = 1, , n, thus x > z. △

Problem 7. Let a, b ∈ R be any two real numbers. Show that the following holds:

| a | − | b | ≤ | a ± b | ≤ | a | + | b |.

Hint: We have shown the following during lectures, which you can take for granted:

| x | ≤ y ⇐⇒ − y ≤ x ≤ y and − | x | ≤ x ≤ | x |.

Using these inequalities, show first that | a + b| ≤ | a| + |b| and then that | a| − |b| ≤ | a − b|. The remaining inequalities, | a| − |b| ≤ | a + b| and | a − b| ≤ | a| + |b| follow easily.

Solution Following the hint, we have that −| a| ≤ a ≤ | a| and −|b| ≤ b ≤ |b|. Adding these obtains − (| a| + |b|) ≤ a + b ≤ | a| + |b|. This is equivalent to | a + b| ≤ | a| + |b|, as desired. Now note that

| a| = |( a − b) + b| ≤ | a − b| + |b|,

and thus | a| − |b| ≤ | a − b|. The other two inequalities follow by noticing that | x | = | − x |. △
